fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If \Sigma \subseteq \Omega were \lnot \lnot-closed, we would have \Sigma = \Omega. Indeed, for any \phi : \Omega it would suffice to show \lnot \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi \in \Omega \right ) \mathclose {}}; because this proposition is negated, we may assume that either \phi = \top or \phi = \bot, both of which lie in \Sigma.
There is a small error in Section 5.1 of Welsey Phoa's doctoral thesis, which we quote below:

fr:blockquote.fr:p: Let \Sigma, the r.e. subobject classifier, be the closure of the decidable truth values 2 = { \mathopen {} \left \{ \top , \bot \right \} \mathclose {}} under countable joins; that is, \Sigma = { \mathopen {} \left \{ p \in \Omega \mid \exists f \in 2 ^{ \mathbb {N} } . \ p \leftrightarrow { \mathopen {} \left ( \exists n.f { \mathopen {} \left ( n \right ) \mathclose {}} = \top \right ) \mathclose {}} \right \} \mathclose {}} It's clearly a sublattice of \Omega. Observe that by Markov's Principle, the truth-values in \Sigma are \lnot \lnot-closed; i.e. \forall p \in \Omega . \lnot \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( p \in \Sigma \right ) \mathclose {}} \to p \in \Sigma So \Sigma \rightarrowtail \nabla2.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The English prose is correct, but it is not accurately reflected in the second mathematical display, which mistakenly asserts not that the truth-values are \lnot \lnot-closed but rather that \Sigma is itself a \lnot \lnot-closed subobject of \Omega. This is not the case.

If \Sigma \subseteq \Omega were \lnot \lnot-closed, we would have \Sigma = \Omega. Indeed, for any \phi : \Omega it would suffice to show \lnot \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi \in \Omega \right ) \mathclose {}}; because this proposition is negated, we may assume that either \phi = \top or \phi = \bot, both of which lie in \Sigma.
I thank Andrew Swan for explaining the argument above to me.
